title: Carolingian minuscule, the alphabet
subtitle: The ninth-century script that gave the Renaissance its lowercase letters
excerpt: Carolingian minuscule was developed during the reign of Charlemagne as a clear, legible script for imperial documents. It is the direct ancestor of the lowercase letterforms used in European printing from Gutenberg onward. The script is rounder and more open than Foundational, with a characteristic slight forward lean.
sourceNotes: The Carolingian script tradition is documented across multiple public-domain paleography resources. Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), discusses the historical development and letterform principles. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john.

glossaryTerms.definition: A standardised script developed at the Carolingian court in the ninth century. Characterised by its rounded forms, clear ascenders and descenders, and forward tilt. The model from which Renaissance humanist scripts and ultimately modern lowercase letterforms derive.
The height of a lowercase letter without ascenders or descenders, measured in nib-widths of the writing nib. The fundamental proportion that determines the weight and character of a script.

content.text: was developed in Frankish scriptoria during Charlemagne's court reforms in the late eighth and ninth centuries. It replaced the regional scripts of the early medieval period with a single clear letterform. In the fifteenth century, Italian humanists chose Carolingian manuscripts as their model, thinking they were classical Roman scripts. That is why Carolingian looks familiar. It is the direct ancestor of the lowercase letters in every Roman typeface.

The script is rounder and more open than Foundational, with a clear forward lean of 3 to 5 degrees and slightly taller proportions. The nib angle is held at approximately 30 to 35 degrees from the baseline, matching Foundational. The difference in character comes from the slight slant and the rounded, open bowl forms.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: The default practice nib for Carolingian. Use Mitchell 2 for larger work, Mitchell 4 for smaller.

items.name: Iron-gall ink (slug: iron-gall-ink)
items.description: Period-appropriate; the Carolingian scriptoria used iron-gall for dark, permanent text. Walnut ink (slug: walnut-ink) gives a warmer brown tone that suits practice work.

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm (slug: cartridge-paper-130)
items.description: For practice. Sized paper or hot-press watercolour paper for finished pieces.

items.name: Tracing paper (slug: tracing-paper)
items.description: As a hand guard to rest the writing hand on.

content.text: is 4 nib-widths (like Foundational). Ascenders rise 3 nib-widths above the x-height line. Descenders drop 3 nib-widths below the baseline. These proportions produce the characteristic open, airy look of the script. Rule guide lines before writing: x-height, cap-height (same as ascender height), baseline, and descender line.

Hold the nib at 30 to 35 degrees from the baseline. The pen rests between the index and middle fingers with the thumb alongside; the hand rests on the side of the little finger. The nib angle does not change for Carolingian the way it does for italic; 30 to 35 degrees is consistent throughout the alphabet.

The letter o is circular and almost perfectly round, slightly forward-tilted. Stroke 1: a clockwise arc from approximately 11 o'clock, sweeping left and down to complete the circle from inside. Stroke 2: the closing arc from 11 o'clock going right and down. The widest point of the o is at the midline.

The letter n is the key model for all arch-based letters (m, h, u, r). Stroke 1: a straight vertical stem from the x-height line to the baseline. Stroke 2: the arch begins at two-thirds of the stem height, curves up and to the right to the x-height line, then comes down as a second vertical. The curve of the arch is rounded, not pointed; Carolingian does not share the pointed arch of textura.

Ascenders carry a small curved serif at the head, angled slightly to the right. Descenders end in a small curved foot, similarly formed. These entry and exit serifs are gentle; the script is not as mechanically geometric as Roman Foundational.

items.symptom: Letters look like Foundational rather than Carolingian
items.cause: The script's slight forward lean not applied; writing too upright
items.fix: Tilt the guide-line sheet 3 to 5 degrees clockwise and write straight up the slanted sheet, or explicitly lean each letter slightly forward.

items.symptom: Arches of n, m, and h look pointed
items.cause: Arch starting too close to the baseline; not sweeping round to the full x-height
items.fix: Begin the arch stroke at two-thirds of the stem height, not halfway. The curve must reach the full x-height before coming down.
